evidence_items.snippet: --- PAGE 1 --- Fax +41 61 306 12 34 E-Mail karger@karger.ch www.karger.com Original Paper Skin Pharmacol Physiol 2009;22:15–21 DOI: 10.1159/000183922 Influence of Ethanol on the Solubility, Ionization and Permeation Characteristics of Ibuprofen in Silicone and Human Skin R.M. Watkinson a C. Herkenne b R.H. Guy b, c J. Hadgraft d G. Oliveira d M.E. Lane d a Victorian College of Pharmacy, Monash University, Parkville, Vic. , Australia; b Debio Recherche Pharmaceutique SA, Martigny , Switzerland; c Departm

ation Skin Pharmacol Physiol 2009;22:15–21 17 ducted with membranes pretreated with ethanol for 24 h to fur- ther investigate the influence of ethanol on ibuprofen perme- ation. Flux values were calculated from the cumulative amount of drug diffusing into the receptor compartment as a function of time. The slope of this profile was determined once steady state had been reached. Values of the permeability coefficient k p , Kh and D/h 2 were calculated by fitting the data to equation 2: (2) 2 2 2 2 2 2 1 1 2 1 exp 6

pecac multiple reflection accessory with a zinc sel- enide crystal was used and data were acquired using OPUS soft- ware, version 5.5 (Bruker, Germany). Results and Discussion Solubility Studies Addition of 25% ethanol increased the solubility of ibuprofen to 343 mg/ml, a 3,800-fold enhancement rela- tive to the drug’s aqueous solubility ( table 1 ). In pure eth- anol, the solubility is 5,500-fold higher than in water. Ibu- profen has a reported pK a of 4.41 [11] , explaining the con- siderable solubility enhanceme

provenance.route_notes: Study systematically investigates ethanol's effects on ibuprofen solubility and permeation.

route_anchor_evidence.snippet: Permeation Studies in Silicone Membrane and Skin
Table 3 and Table 5 contain steady-state fluxes for ibuprofen permeation.
Table 1 and Table 2 outline the solubility of ibuprofen in different formulations.

evidence_items.snippet: Steady-state flux values and permeability coefficients for the permeation of ibuprofen from saturated formulations containing MO and MG 812 through human epidermis.
Steady-state flux values and permeability coefficients for the permeation of ibuprofen from saturated formulations containing MO and MG 812 through human epidermis.

Steady-state flux values and permeability coefficients for the permeation of ibuprofen from saturated formulations containing MO and MG 812 through human epidermis. --- PAGE 1 --- Fax +41 61 306 12 34 E-Mail karger@karger.ch www.karger.com Original Paper Skin Pharmacol Physiol 2011;24:22–26 DOI: 10.1159/000315139 Optimisation of Cosolvent Concentration for Topical Drug Delivery III – Influence of Lipophi

Steady-state flux values and permeability coefficients for the permeation of ibuprofen from saturated formulations containing MO and MG 812 through human epidermis. --- PAGE 1 --- Fax +41 61 306 12 34 E-Mail karger@karger.ch www.karger.com Original Paper Skin Pharmacol Physiol 2011;24:22–26 DOI: 10.1159/000315139 Optimisation of Cosolvent Concentration for Topical Drug Delivery III – Influence of Lipophi

osted and cut to the appropriate size. Permeation experiments were con- ducted with saturated solutions of ibuprofen for each vehicle us- ing Franz diffusion cells with dimensions previously reported [4] . For all permeation experiments the receptor fluid was pH 7.4 phosphate-buffered saline and sink conditions were maintained at all time points. The diffusion experiments were performed at 32 ° C under occluded conditions by sealing the donor compart- ment with microscope cover slips. The number of replicates was a

rd B, Hadgraft J, Hoel- gaard A: Vehicle effect on topical drug deliv- ery. III. Effect of azone on the cutaneous per- meation of metronidazole and propylene glycol. Int J Pharm 1985; 24: 19–26. 2 Trottet L, Merly C, Mirza M, Hadgraft J, Da- vis AF: Effect of finite doses of propylene gly- col on enhancement of in vitro percutaneous permeation of loperamide hydrochloride. Int J Pharm 2004; 274: 213–219. 3 Otto A, Wiechers JW, Kelly CL, Hadgraft J, du Plessis J: Effect of penetration modifiers on the dermal and tran

00 31.9983.44 Table 2. Vehicle solubility parameter and uptake by silicone mem- brane and skin at 32° C (mean 8 SD, n = 3) Vehicle Solubility parameter (cal/cm3)1/2 S olvent uptake, % (w/w) si licone skin MO 7.1 9.580.6* 20.585.9 MO/MG (50/50) 8.3 9.681.1* 14.189.8 MG 9.4 2.380.3 21.984.4 Water 23.4 1.680.8 89.8822.6 * p ≤ 0.05: significantly different from MG and water. --- PAGE 4 --- Optimisation of Cosolvent Concentration for Topical Drug Delivery Skin Pharmacol Physiol 2011;24:22–26 25 Permeation Studies in Sk

provenance.route_notes: Study extends prior work on hydrophilic systems to lipophilic vehicles with a focus on ibuprofen.
